family_history: No family history of epilepsy, autoimmune disease, or psychiatric illness

social_history.alcohol: Occasional social drinking, 1-2 drinks per week
social_history.living_situation: Lives with partner in an apartment

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert but disoriented to date and place. Intermittently agitated, with periods of unresponsiveness lasting 20-30 seconds. Speech is disorganized with paraphasic errors and echolalia. Unable to follow multi-step commands. Montreal Cognitive Assessment attempted but patient unable to cooperate. Short-term memory severely impaired; unable to recall any of 3 objects at 5 minutes.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal and reactive, 3 mm bilaterally. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact to light touch. Mild bilateral facial weakness noted with decreased nasolabial fold depth. Hearing grossly intact. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline. Prominent orofacial dyskinesias with repetitive lip-smacking and jaw movements observed throughout the examination.
neurological_exam.motor: Tone increased in bilateral upper extremities with intermittent rigidity. Strength 4/5 throughout when patient cooperates, though limited by inconsistent effort due to encephalopathy. No frank hemiparesis. Intermittent dystonic posturing of the left hand observed.
neurological_exam.sensory: Withdraws symmetrically to pain in all four extremities. Detailed sensory examination limited by encephalopathy.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 3+ bilaterally, Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Unable to perform finger-to-nose or heel-to-shin testing reliably due to encephalopathy and involuntary movements.
neurological_exam.gait: Unable to assess; patient is bed-bound due to altered mental status and agitation.
neurological_exam.additional: Prominent orofacial dyskinesias with stereotyped lip-smacking, tongue protrusion, and jaw movements. Intermittent choreoathetoid movements of the upper extremities bilaterally. Episodes of autonomic instability observed during examination including transient tachycardia to 130 bpm and blood pressure fluctuations. Two brief episodes of staring with behavioral arrest lasting approximately 15 seconds each observed during the encounter, concerning for subclinical seizures.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive confusion, seizures, and abnormal movements over two weeks
patient.history_present_illness: This 24-year-old right-handed woman was brought to the emergency department by her partner and mother after two weeks of progressively worsening behavioral changes, confusion, and new-onset seizures. Approximately three weeks ago, the patient developed a mild upper respiratory illness with low-grade fevers, sore throat, malaise, and rhinorrhea lasting 4-5 days, for which she did not seek medical attention. She appeared to recover, but approximately one week later her partner noticed personality changes: she became increasingly irritable, anxious, and paranoid, expressing unfounded beliefs that her classmates were plotting against her. She stopped attending her graduate classes and became withdrawn. Over the following days, she developed progressive confusion, insomnia, and difficulty finding words. Five days prior to presentation, she had her first witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting approximately 90 seconds with post-ictal confusion lasting 30 minutes. She was taken to an outside emergency department where she was given lorazepam 2 mg IV and discharged with a referral to neurology. Over the subsequent days, her condition deteriorated rapidly. She developed repetitive lip-smacking and jaw movements that her family initially mistook for a medication side effect. She became increasingly agitated with periods of unresponsiveness alternating with combative episodes. On the day of presentation, her partner witnessed a second generalized seizure and called emergency services. In the emergency department, the patient was noted to have fluctuating levels of consciousness, prominent orofacial dyskinesias, intermittent tachycardia, and blood pressure lability. She was unable to provide a coherent history. Her partner reports no prior psychiatric history, no recent travel, no tick bites, no new medications, and no illicit drug use.

findings.frequency: 1-2 Hz delta activity with superimposed 20-30 Hz beta
findings.morphology: Rhythmic delta waves with overriding fast activity resembling a brush pattern
findings.state: Present in both wakefulness and drowsiness
findings.clinical_correlation: Extreme delta brush pattern is highly characteristic of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis

findings.location: Left temporal onset with secondary generalization
findings.frequency: Two subclinical electrographic seizures captured during 30-minute recording
findings.morphology: Rhythmic theta evolving to repetitive sharp waves
findings.clinical_correlation: Subclinical seizures consistent with seizure burden in autoimmune encephalitis
Reflects diffuse cortical dysfunction consistent with encephalitis

artifacts.description: Intermittent movement artifact related to orofacial dyskinesias and upper extremity choreoathetoid movements

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to patient's inability to cooperate
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: No photic driving response; no photoparoxysmal response

eeg.impression: Severely abnormal EEG showing extreme delta brush pattern, generalized rhythmic delta activity, and two electrographic seizures of left temporal origin. The extreme delta brush pattern is highly characteristic of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis. The overall pattern indicates diffuse cortical dysfunction with epileptogenic activity.
eeg.limitations: Recording limited to 30 minutes; continuous EEG monitoring recommended to assess full seizure burden
recommended_actions: Continuous EEG monitoring for at least 24-48 hours

findings.location: Bilateral medial temporal lobes, more prominent on the left
findings.size: Left: 2.1 x 1.4 cm, Right: 1.3 x 0.9 cm

additional_observations: Ventricles normal in size and configuration
mri.impression: Bilateral medial temporal FLAIR hyperintensities without restricted diffusion, enhancement, or mass effect, left greater than right. Subtle bilateral insular cortical signal abnormality. The distribution and signal characteristics are most consistent with autoimmune or viral encephalitis. The absence of restricted diffusion and hemorrhagic changes makes HSV encephalitis less likely but does not exclude it.

differential_by_imaging.rationale: Bilateral medial temporal involvement without hemorrhage or restricted diffusion favors autoimmune over infectious etiology
Temporal lobe involvement but typically unilateral, hemorrhagic, with restricted diffusion
Cannot be distinguished from antibody-mediated autoimmune encephalitis by imaging alone

recommended_actions: CSF analysis including autoimmune encephalitis antibody panel
Consider repeat MRI in 1-2 weeks if clinical course evolves
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis for occult malignancy if autoimmune encephalitis confirmed

ecg.interpretation: Sinus tachycardia at 112 bpm. No acute ischemic changes. QTc at upper limit of normal.
ecg.clinical_correlation: Tachycardia likely related to autonomic instability in the setting of encephalitis and agitation.

BMP.clinical_significance: Hyponatremia, consider SIADH in the setting of CNS pathology

Other.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated, likely related to seizure activity and rigidity

labs.interpretation: Mild leukocytosis, hyponatremia (131 mEq/L) suggestive of SIADH, mildly elevated inflammatory markers, and mildly elevated CK likely from seizure activity. Normal thyroid function argues against Hashimoto encephalopathy. Negative urine drug screen rules out drug-induced psychosis. Normal hepatic and renal function.

special_tests.Oligoclonal bands: Positive (6 bands, not present in serum)

csf.interpretation: Lymphocytic pleocytosis (65 cells/uL, 95% lymphocytes) with mildly elevated protein and normal glucose ratio. Oligoclonal bands present with elevated IgG index indicating intrathecal antibody synthesis. All viral PCRs negative, ruling out common viral encephalitides. Anti-NMDA receptor antibodies positive at high titer (1:320), confirming the diagnosis of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis.

NMDAR Antibody Panel.clinical_significance: Confirms systemic anti-NMDA receptor antibody production
CSF titer higher than serum, confirming intrathecal production and supporting the diagnosis of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis

output.interpretation: Both serum and CSF anti-NMDA receptor antibodies are positive. The CSF titer (1:320) exceeds the serum titer (1:160), indicating intrathecal antibody production, which is the hallmark of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis. This confirms the diagnosis.

Pelvic Ultrasound.value: 3.2 x 2.1 x 2.5 cm, contains a 1.8 cm complex mass with mixed solid and cystic components, including echogenic foci suggesting calcification or fat (dermoid/teratoma). Doppler shows mild internal vascularity.
Pelvic Ultrasound.clinical_significance: Ovarian teratoma identified; associated with anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis in up to 50% of young women

Pelvic Ultrasound.value: Normal, 2.8 x 1.9 x 2.0 cm, follicular pattern appropriate for age
Normal size and echotexture, endometrial stripe 6 mm
Trace physiologic free fluid in cul-de-sac

output.interpretation: A 1.8 cm complex mass in the right ovary with mixed solid-cystic components and echogenic foci consistent with a mature cystic teratoma (dermoid cyst). This finding is clinically significant as ovarian teratomas are the most common tumor trigger for anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis. Surgical resection is recommended as part of treatment, as tumor removal improves neurological outcomes and reduces relapse risk.
abnormal_values_summary: Right ovary: 1.8 cm complex mass consistent with mature cystic teratoma

output.interpretation: The comprehensive autoimmune encephalitis panel confirms isolated anti-NMDA receptor antibody positivity. All other neuronal surface antibodies (LGI1, CASPR2, GABA-B, AMPA, DPPX) are negative. Anti-TPO antibodies are normal, effectively excluding Hashimoto encephalopathy. ANA is negative. These results support a diagnosis of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis without evidence of overlap syndromes.

signal_characteristics.density: Mixed density with fat (-40 HU) and calcification components

output.impression: A 2.0 cm well-circumscribed right ovarian mass with fat and calcification components, consistent with a mature cystic teratoma (dermoid cyst). No evidence of other primary malignancy, lymphadenopathy, or metastatic disease elsewhere. The teratoma is likely the immunologic trigger for the patient's anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis.

differential_by_imaging.rationale: Fat and calcification in a well-circumscribed ovarian mass is characteristic
Well-circumscribed margins and small size argue against immature variant

recommended_actions: Gynecologic oncology consultation for surgical resection

special_tests.Oligoclonal bands: Positive (6 bands, not present in serum, unchanged from prior LP)

output.interpretation: Repeat lumbar puncture shows persistent lymphocytic pleocytosis and elevated protein, essentially unchanged from the initial LP. Anti-NMDA receptor antibody titer remains at 1:320. Persistent inflammatory CSF profile is expected early in the disease course and does not imply treatment failure at this stage. Serial CSF monitoring will be important to assess treatment response over weeks to months.

Pre-Rituximab Screening.clinical_significance: Safe to proceed; HBV reactivation risk low
No latent TB; safe for immunosuppression
Baseline IgG within normal limits; monitor for hypogammaglobulinemia with rituximab

output.interpretation: Pre-rituximab screening labs are reassuring. Hepatitis B and C serologies are negative, reducing risk of viral reactivation with B-cell depletion therapy. QuantiFERON is negative. Baseline IgG is normal. Patient may proceed with rituximab if first-line immunotherapy (IVIG/steroids/plasma exchange) is insufficient.

Osmolality Studies.clinical_significance: Inappropriately concentrated urine relative to low serum osmolality, consistent with SIADH
Elevated urine sodium in the setting of hyponatremia supports SIADH

output.interpretation: The combination of hypotonic hyponatremia (serum osmolality 268 mOsm/kg), inappropriately concentrated urine (520 mOsm/kg), and elevated urine sodium (45 mEq/L) is diagnostic of the syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion (SIADH). SIADH is a recognized complication of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis. Fluid restriction is recommended. Monitor sodium closely.
abnormal_values_summary: Urine sodium 45 mEq/L (elevated in setting of hyponatremia)

differential.key_distinguishing: Temporal lobe predilection on MRI with hemorrhagic changes, acute high fevers, positive CSF HSV PCR. In this case, HSV PCR is negative, MRI lacks hemorrhagic changes, and the clinical course is subacute rather than hyperacute.
No seizures, no movement disorder, no autonomic instability, normal EEG and MRI. This patient has seizures, orofacial dyskinesias, autonomic instability, severely abnormal EEG, and CSF pleocytosis.
Requires exposure to antipsychotics, characterized by lead-pipe rigidity, very high CK (>1000), high fever. Patient has no antipsychotic exposure and CK is only mildly elevated.
Anti-TPO antibodies markedly elevated, often with thyroid dysfunction. This patient has normal thyroid function and normal anti-TPO antibodies.
Multifocal infarcts on MRI, vessel irregularities on angiography. This patient has no ischemic changes on MRI.

optimal_actions.action: Stabilize patient: secure airway, establish IV access, continuous monitoring. Administer IV benzodiazepine (lorazepam 2 mg) if active seizure.
Obtain urgent labs: CBC, BMP, CRP, CK, liver function, thyroid function, urine drug screen, blood cultures
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Hyponatremia suggesting SIADH, mildly elevated CK and inflammatory markers, normal thyroid function, negative drug screen

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with and without contrast
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Bilateral medial temporal FLAIR hyperintensities without hemorrhage or restricted diffusion

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture for CSF analysis including cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, cultures, viral PCRs, oligoclonal bands, and anti-NMDA receptor antibodies
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein, oligoclonal bands, positive anti-NMDA receptor antibodies

optimal_actions.action: Start empiric IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg q8h pending HSV PCR results
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Empiric treatment until HSV can be ruled out
Extreme delta brush pattern, subclinical electrographic seizures, generalized slowing

optimal_actions.action: Start anti-seizure medication (levetiracetam 1000 mg IV q12h)
Once anti-NMDAR antibodies confirmed positive: initiate first-line immunotherapy with IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily x 5 days AND IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day x 5 days
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Initiation of immunotherapy to reduce antibody-mediated neuronal injury

optimal_actions.action: Order pelvic ultrasound to screen for ovarian teratoma
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Ovarian teratoma identified, requiring surgical resection

optimal_actions.action: Consult gynecologic oncology for teratoma resection
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Surgical planning for ovarian teratoma removal to eliminate immunologic trigger

optimal_actions.action: If insufficient clinical improvement after first-line immunotherapy: escalate to second-line therapy with rituximab 375 mg/m2 weekly x 4 doses
Fluid restriction for SIADH management with serial sodium monitoring

critical_actions: Perform lumbar puncture and send CSF for anti-NMDA receptor antibodies
Start empiric acyclovir until HSV PCR returns negative
Initiate first-line immunotherapy (IV steroids and IVIG or plasma exchange) once diagnosis confirmed
Screen for ovarian teratoma with pelvic imaging
Initiate anti-seizure medication for seizure control
Continuous EEG monitoring to assess subclinical seizure burden
Admit to ICU or step-down unit for close monitoring of autonomic instability
contraindicated_actions: Diagnosing as primary psychiatric disorder without excluding autoimmune encephalitis
Administering high-dose antipsychotics, which can worsen dyskinesias and mimic neuroleptic malignant syndrome
Delaying immunotherapy while waiting for antibody results if clinical suspicion is high
Performing brain biopsy, which is unnecessary when anti-NMDA receptor antibodies are positive
Rapid sodium correction (risk of osmotic demyelination syndrome in the setting of SIADH)
key_reasoning_points: The combination of subacute psychiatric symptoms followed by seizures, orofacial dyskinesias, and autonomic instability in a young woman is the classic anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis phenotype
Extreme delta brush on EEG is pathognomonic for anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis and is not seen in other encephalitides
Viral prodrome preceding neuropsychiatric symptoms is a recognized feature and should raise suspicion for autoimmune encephalitis
Hyponatremia from SIADH is a common complication of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis and CNS inflammation
Ovarian teratoma is found in up to 50% of young women with anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis; tumor resection is essential for treatment and reduces relapse risk
CSF anti-NMDA receptor antibodies are more sensitive than serum antibodies; CSF testing is mandatory
HSV encephalitis must be empirically treated until PCR results are available, as delayed treatment carries high morbidity
First-line immunotherapy should include high-dose IV corticosteroids combined with IVIG or plasma exchange
